attempts.test_code:
from source import gcd

def test_gcd_reveals_infinite_recursion():
    """
    This test exposes the bug where gcd(a, b) calls gcd(a % b, b) instead of
    gcd(b, a % b). With inputs (35, 21), the recursion becomes infinite:
      gcd(35,21) -> gcd(14,21) -> gcd(14,21) -> ...
    Because a % b < b in the second call, the arguments never change.

    On the buggy implementation, this will raise a RecursionError (or hang)
    and the test will fail. On the fixed version, it correctly returns 7.
    """
    assert gcd(35, 21) == 7, (
        "gcd(35, 21) should equal 7, but the buggy code either loops "
        "infinitely or raises RecursionError due to incorrect argument order."
    )
